title: practical english usage - Part 24
content:
indirect object + direct object. The  structure is not possible, for example, with Not all verbs that refer to transferring or communicating can be followed by and   take 6 structures        with    donate,        push,      carry,      explain,        suggest,         describe For details of these passive structures,    61.

’ What happened to the stuff he left behind?' ‘ Well, the picture was given to However, the thing which  is given, sent, etc can be the subject if necessary.

We were all bought little presents.

I've just been given a lovely picture.

person who  receives something, not the thing which  is sent, given, etc.

When  these verbs are used in passive structures, the subject is usually the 5  passives:     I've  been      given      a  picture.

Who was it sent to? (     Who was it sent?) Prepositions are used in wh-questions referring to the indirect object.

4 wh-questions:        Who      did    you     buy     it  for?

(e.g. He gave you it or Send them them) are often felt to be unnatural.

However, this structure is avoided in some cases: phrases ending with it or them Give her one. Send him  some.

It is also possible to put the indirect object first.

Lend them to her. Send some to him. Give it (to) me.

To is occasionally dropped after it in informal British English.

When  both objects are pronouns, it is common to put the indirect object last.

3 two    pronouns:      Lend     them      to   her.

(Not all grammars call these prepositional phrases ’ indirect objects’ .) Mother bought the ice cream for you, not for me.

Mrs Norman sent some flowers to the nurse.

I gave the keys to Jack. I handed my licence to the police officer.

has a preposition (usually to or for).

We can also put the indirect object after the direct object. In this case it normally 2  indirect      object     last:  I gave      the    keys     to   Jack.

Not all verbs with this kind of meaning can be used like this, ►    8.6.

Throw me the ball. We wish you a Merry Christmas.

grammar * 9  verbs with both active and passive meanings The handle won't unscrew - can you help me?

The carpet's made of a special material that doesn't stain.

These glasses are so fragile: they break if you look at them.

Be careful what you put on the table - it scratches easily. (= You can easily to materials: e.g. bend, break, crack, melt, polish, scratch, stain, tear, unscrew.

The  intransitive structure is used with many verbs referring to things we  can do 2 It scratches          easily.

The car won't start.

- I can't start the car.

Suddenly she woke.

- Something woke her.

Your book's selling well.

- We're selling a lot of copies of your book.

She's changed a lot since she got married.

- Marriage has really changed her.

The curtain's moving.

- The wind's moving the curtain.

The door opened.

- She opened the door.

reflexive (►   178) verb. Compare: subject. The  intransitive use has a meaning rather like a passive (►   57) or Some verbs are used transitively and intransitively with different kinds of 1 She     opened        the    door.      / The     door      opened.

passive      meanings 9 verbs     with    both    active     and For structures with object complements (e.g. They made him  captain), ►     10.

(More common than Write me .. . in standard British Write me a letter. Write to me when you get home.

Sing her a song.          Sing to her. (     Sing her.) object. Compare: when sing, play and write have no direct object, we  put to before the indirect Other verbs like this include teach, tell, pay, show, sing, play and write. Note that I asked Jack. I asked a question. I asked Jack a question.

both.

Some verbs can be followed by either a direct object, or an indirect object, or 7 one   object     or  two not I took her some money. (= ɪ  took some money to her -       ... from her.) but not 'take from'.

Take can be used with indirect object + direct object when it means 'take to' 1 Verbs    Section grammar •   10   verb + object + complement:  You make me nervous.

The doctors found her to be perfectly fit.

- Everybody found her very pleasant.
